outcome_label: cutting back the amount of consumed meat
outcome_description: Participants were asked a multiple-choice question regarding whether they try to cut back on the amount of meat they eat. They could select from the following options: (a) Yes, I am actively trying to cut back; (b) No, but I have cut back on meat in the past; (c) No, but I am considering cutting back; (d) No, I am not considering cutting back (skip to question 6B); (e) I do not eat meat; (f) I'm not sure. The outcome represents the percentage of participants trying to cut back on their meat consumption.

Participants were asked to what extent they intend to change their harmful behaviour regarding animals. They could indicate separately for different categories (9 items), and choose their intent to show this behavior "more", "equal", "less", "not at all" for future.

Participants' reported meat consumption via PrimeScreen Dietary Screening Tool. This tool was a 17-item questionnaire that assesses the frequency that respondents consume various foods.  In cohort 1, participants responded to the questionnaire using the original scale: 0 = never, 1 = less than once per week, 2 = once per week, 3 = 2–4 times per week, 4 = nearly daily or daily, and 5 = twice or more daily

Participants' reported meat consumption via PrimeScreen Dietary Screening Tool. This tool was a 17-item questionnaire that assesses the frequency that respondents consume various foods. In cohort 2, the authors modified the scale for clarity as follows: 0 = never/rarely, 1 = 1–3 times a month, 2 = 1–4 times a week, 3 = daily/almost daily, and 4 = twice or more daily

outcome_label: amount of chili con carne taken
outcome_description: The amont of chili con carne (in grams) that participants took from the buffet.

Participants were asked to rate plant-based diets using four semantic differential items on a 7-point scale: “bad” to “good”, “harmful” to “beneficial”, “unpleasant” to “pleasant”, “unenjoyable” to “enjoyable”
